meta.title: 50 Important Java Programs for ICSE Class 10 Boards
meta.description: The complete ICSE Class 10 Java program bank: 50 board-style programs in 7 families, every one compiled and run, with its real output shown.
keywords: java programs for icse board exam

author.name: Modern Age Coders
author.bio: Expert educators passionate about making coding accessible and fun for learners of all ages.

meta.tldr: This is a complete Java program bank for the ICSE Class 10 boards: 50 board style programs grouped into seven families, from numbers and patterns to strings, arrays and matrices. Every program is compiled and run with its real output shown, so students practise the exact question styles the exam uses.

hero.title: 50 Important Java Programs for ICSE Class 10 Boards
hero.subtitle: The complete revision bank: seven program families the paper draws from, 50 programs, every one compiled, executed, and shown with its real output.

featuredImage.alt: 50 important Java programs for the ICSE Class 10 board exam with real outputs

sections.text: Ask any ICSE Computer Applications teacher what separates a 95 from a 70, and you will hear the same answer: the students who score are the ones who have already written the standard programs so many times that the exam feels like revision. This page is that practice, in one place: 50 Java programs for ICSE Class 10, organised into the seven families the paper draws from.

Every single program below was compiled and executed before being published, and the output under each one is the real output it printed. Each uses sample values written directly into the code so you can run it the moment you paste it into BlueJ. Where an exam question says accept from the user, wrap the same logic in a Scanner, the logic is the mark-earning part.
If 50 feels like a lot, start with our Top 20 for ICSE, which walks the highest-frequency picks slowly with full explanations. This page is the complete bank: broader, faster, built for revision.
How the 50 Are Organised

In the ICSE paper, Section B carries 60 of the 100 marks and is where full programs are written, with a choice of questions. The seven families below are how those questions cluster, and knowing which family a question belongs to is half of solving it.

sections.alt: The 50 ICSE Java programs organised into seven families: conditions, loops and series, special numbers, patterns, strings, arrays, and methods with constructors
sections.caption: Seven families, 50 programs. Recognise the family and you already know the shape of the answer.

sections.title: The recognition habit
sections.text: Before writing any exam answer, name the family out loud: this is a special number, so it is digit extraction plus one comparison. This is a pattern, so outer loop rows, inner loop columns. Students who classify first almost never freeze.
Conditions and Operators
Eight warm-up programs built on if, else, and the operators. Section A loves these as output questions, and Section B uses them as the opening steps of bigger programs.
1. Even or odd
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is even or odd.

sections.code: 47 is odd

sections.text: 2. Largest of three numbers
Question: Write a program to find the largest of three numbers using nested if.

sections.code: Largest = 62

sections.text: 3. Positive, negative or zero
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is positive, negative, or zero.

sections.code: Negative

sections.text: 4. Leap year check
Question: Write a program to check whether a year is a leap year.

sections.code: 2028 is a leap year

sections.text: 5. Grade from marks (if-else ladder)
Question: Write a program to print the grade for given marks: A for 80 and above, B for 60 to 79, C for 40 to 59, else D.

sections.code: Marks 73 gets grade B

sections.text: 6. Simple interest
Question: Write a program to calculate simple interest for a given principal, rate, and time.

sections.code: Simple Interest = 800.0

sections.text: 7. Celsius to Fahrenheit
Question: Write a program to convert a temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit.
8. Buzz number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Buzz number. A buzz number ends with 7 or is divisible by 7.

sections.code: 63 is a Buzz number

sections.text: Loops and Series
The while and for loop workhorses. Master the digit-extraction pair here, sum of digits and reverse, because half the special numbers below reuse it.
9. Sum of first n natural numbers
Question: Write a program to find the sum of the first n natural numbers.

sections.code: Sum of first 50 numbers = 1275

sections.text: 10. Factorial of a number
Question: Write a program to find the factorial of a number using a loop.
11. Multiplication table
Question: Write a program to print the multiplication table of a number.
12. Fibonacci series
Question: Write a program to print the first n terms of the Fibonacci series.
13. Sum of the series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ...
Question: Write a program to find the sum of the series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... + 1/n.
14. Sum of digits
Question: Write a program to find the sum of the digits of a number.

sections.code: Sum of digits of 4718 = 20

sections.text: 15. Reverse a number
Question: Write a program to reverse the digits of a number.

sections.code: Reverse of 8256 = 6528

sections.text: 16. Count the digits
Question: Write a program to count how many digits a number has.

sections.code: 90387 has 5 digits

sections.text: Special Numbers
The most ICSE family of them all. Ten number types, each with a one-line definition and a worked check. Notice how nearly all of them are digit extraction wearing different hats.

sections.alt: How to recognise which family an ICSE Java question belongs to from its wording
sections.caption: Question words that give the family away. Classify first, then write.

sections.text: 17. Prime number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is prime.

sections.code: 97 is prime

sections.text: 18. Palindrome number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a palindrome.

sections.code: 1331 is a palindrome

sections.text: 19. Armstrong number
Question: Write a program to check whether a 3-digit number is an Armstrong number.

sections.code: 371 is an Armstrong number

sections.text: 20. Perfect number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Perfect number.

sections.code: 496 is a Perfect number

sections.text: 21. Automorphic number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is Automorphic. Its square must end with the number itself.

sections.code: 76 is Automorphic (square = 5776)

sections.text: 22. Neon number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Neon number. The digits of its square must add up to the number.

sections.code: 9 is a Neon number

sections.text: 23. Spy number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Spy number. The sum of its digits must equal the product of its digits.

sections.code: 1412 is a Spy number

sections.text: 24. Disarium number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Disarium number. Digits raised to their positions must add up to the number.

sections.code: 175 is a Disarium number

sections.text: 25. Kaprekar number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Kaprekar number. Split its square so the parts add back to the number.

sections.code: 45 is a Kaprekar number (20 + 25)

sections.text: 26. Harshad number
Question: Write a program to check whether a number is a Harshad number. It must be divisible by the sum of its digits.

sections.code: 108 is a Harshad number

sections.text: Patterns with Nested Loops
One pattern appears in almost every paper. The outer loop counts rows, the inner loop decides what each row holds. Six shapes cover every variant examiners rotate through.
27. Right triangle of stars
Question: Write a program to print a right-angled triangle of stars with 5 rows.
28. Inverted star triangle
Question: Write a program to print an inverted right-angled triangle of stars.
29. Star pyramid
Question: Write a program to print a centred pyramid of stars with 5 rows.
30. Number triangle
Question: Write a program to print a triangle where row i contains the numbers 1 to i.
31. Floyd's triangle
Question: Write a program to print Floyd's triangle with 4 rows.
32. Alphabet pattern
Question: Write a program to print a triangle where row i contains the first i capital letters.

sections.code:
A
A B
A B C
A B C D
A B C D E

sections.text: String Programs
String questions carry heavy marks and reward method knowledge: charAt, substring, split, and the Character checks. Piglatin and initials are perennial board favourites.
33. Count vowels
Question: Write a program to count the vowels in a string.

sections.code: Vowels = 8

sections.text: 34. Reverse a string
Question: Write a program to reverse a string using a loop.

sections.code: Reverse of BLUEJ is JEULB

sections.text: 35. Palindrome string
Question: Write a program to check whether a word is a palindrome, ignoring case.

sections.code: Madam is a palindrome

sections.text: 36. Toggle the case
Question: Write a program to change every uppercase letter to lowercase and vice versa.

sections.code: iCsE bOaRdS

sections.text: 37. Count words
Question: Write a program to count the words in a sentence.

sections.code: Words = 6

sections.text: 38. Initials of a name
Question: Write a program to print the initials of a name followed by the surname, like S. K. Gupta.

sections.code: S. K. Gupta

sections.text: 39. Frequency of a character
Question: Write a program to count how many times a given character appears in a string.

sections.code: 'm' appears 2 times

sections.text: 40. Piglatin form
Question: Write a program to convert a word into its Piglatin form: move letters up to the first vowel to the end and add AY.

sections.code: LONDON -> ONDONLAY

sections.text: Array Programs
Single dimensional arrays: search it, sort it, sum it. Bubble and selection sort plus both searches are explicitly on the syllabus, so all four live here.

sections.alt: A five week plan to practise all 50 ICSE Java programs before the board exam
sections.caption: Ten programs a week is a calm pace. Week six is for rewriting the ones that fought back.

sections.text: 41. Largest and smallest
Question: Write a program to find the largest and smallest element in an array.

sections.code:
Largest = 95
Smallest = 12

sections.text: 42. Sum and average
Question: Write a program to find the sum and average of the elements of an array.

sections.code:
Sum = 395
Average = 79.0

sections.text: 43. Linear search
Question: Write a program to search for a value in an array using linear search.

sections.code: 39 found at index 3

sections.text: 44. Binary search
Question: Write a program to search a sorted array using binary search.

sections.code: 38 found at index 3

sections.text: 45. Bubble sort
Question: Write a program to sort an array in ascending order using bubble sort.
46. Selection sort
Question: Write a program to sort an array in ascending order using selection sort.
47. Reverse an array
Question: Write a program to print the elements of an array in reverse order.
Methods, Constructors, and Menus
The paper always tests whether you can shape code, not just write it: overloaded methods, a class with a constructor, and the switch statement.
48. Overloaded area() methods
Question: Write overloaded methods area() to compute the area of a circle, a rectangle, and a triangle, and call all three.

sections.code:
Circle    = 153.86
Rectangle = 60.0
Triangle  = 40.0

sections.text: 49. A class with a constructor
Question: Define a class Student with a parameterised constructor that stores a name and marks, and a method to display them. Create two objects.

sections.code:
Asha scored 92
Rohan scored 85

sections.text: 50. Menu with switch
Question: Write a menu-driven program using switch to print whether a stored choice selects square or cube of a number.

sections.code: Cube of 6 = 216

sections.text: Turning Sample Values into User Input
Every program above uses fixed sample values so it runs instantly. When the question demands input, the swap is mechanical: import java.util.Scanner, create one, and replace the assignment.
That is the entire difference. Practise the logic with fixed values, and add the Scanner shell in the exam when the wording asks for it.
The Marks You Lose Without Noticing

items: Forgetting to reset before reuse: if you reverse a number and then need it again, keep the original in a temp variable. Half of all special-number bugs are this.
Integer division surprises: sum / count truncates when both are int. Cast one side to double for averages.
Comparing strings with ==: use equals() or equalsIgnoreCase(). The == operator compares references, not text.
Off-by-one in loops: arrays run 0 to length - 1. Writing i <= arr.length throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
Missing break in switch: without it, every case below the match also runs.

sections.title: The examiner's eye
sections.text: Presentation earns quiet marks: meaningful variable names, consistent indentation, and one comment naming the logic. Two programs with identical logic do not always score identically.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.title: Are these 50 programs enough for the ICSE board exam?
items.content: They cover every program family the Computer Applications paper draws from: conditions, loops, special numbers, patterns, strings, arrays, and methods with constructors. Pair them with past-paper output questions and theory revision, and the program-writing side is fully prepared.

items.title: Which programs are asked most often in ICSE Class 10?
items.content: Special numbers (Armstrong, palindrome, prime, automorphic, spy, and their cousins), one nested-loop pattern, one or two string programs like Piglatin or initials, and an array program such as bubble sort or binary search appear again and again across years.

items.title: Why do these programs use fixed values instead of Scanner input?
items.content: So every program runs the moment you paste it into BlueJ, and so the output shown could be captured from a real execution. Converting any of them to user input is a three-line Scanner change, shown in the section above.

items.title: How should I practise 50 programs before boards?
items.content: Ten a week for five weeks, typing each one rather than reading it, then a final week rewriting from memory the ones that resisted. Classify every program by family as you go; recognition is what the exam actually tests.

items.title: What is the difference between this list and your Top 20 ICSE post?
items.content: The Top 20 walks the highest-frequency programs slowly, with step-by-step explanations and Scanner-based input. This page is the full revision bank: 50 programs, faster pace, sample values, built for the weeks before the exam.

items.title: Do I need to memorise all the special number definitions?
items.content: Learn the definitions, not the code. Every special number program is digit extraction plus one comparison, so once you can write the extraction loop from memory, each definition takes only a line or two more.

items.title: Which sorting and searching methods does ICSE expect?
items.content: Bubble sort and selection sort for sorting, linear search and binary search for searching, all on single dimensional arrays. All four are in the array family above, executed with their outputs.

sections.text: Fifty Programs, One Calm Exam Hall

There is nothing mysterious about a strong Computer Applications score. It is the same seven families, practised until the pen moves on its own. Work the bank, use the Top 20 walkthroughs when a family needs slower treatment, and keep our string handling guide beside the string family, since that is where the richest marks hide.

And if you want a teacher who has coached this exact paper, our Java classes for ICSE students run live, with learners aged 6 to 67 across our courses. A free demo is the fastest way to see the difference feedback makes.
